text: End of day. Before you close out — take 3 minutes. Not to review your tasks. To notice how you showed up. There are no right answers here. Just honest ones.
Axis 1 of 3: Locus. We're going to look at how you related to today's events — not what happened, but how you positioned yourself inside what happened.
Something didn't go as planned today. When you replay that moment — what's the clearest thought you remember having?
options: [{"label": "I kept trying to figure out what I could actually change", "target": "A1_R1_INT", "signal": "axis1:internal"}, {"label": "I kept noticing what was working against me", "target": "A1_R1_EXT", "signal": "axis1:external"}, {"label": "I moved past it quickly — dwelling felt unproductive", "target": "A1_R1_INT", "signal": "axis1:internal"}, {"label": "I felt like the situation had a logic I couldn't override", "target": "A1_R1_EXT", "signal": "axis1:external"}, {"label": "I wasn't sure if there was anything to do differently", "target": "A1_R1_MID", "signal": "axis1:external"}]

text: You stayed in contact with your own influence — even in a situation that had friction. That's not optimism. It's a specific skill: locating the edge of what you can shape. Notice it without over-crediting yourself.

When conditions feel heavy, that weight is real. What's worth examining is whether the weight was in the situation — or in the interpretation of it. Sometimes both are true, and the distinction matters more than it seems.

Uncertainty about your own agency is honest — and surprisingly common. It often means the situation was genuinely constrained. The question isn't whether you had full control. It's whether you noticed the partial control you did have.
You identified where you had influence. What did you actually do with that awareness?
options: [{"label": "I made a decision and acted on it, even if imperfectly", "target": "A1_D1", "signal": "axis1:internal"}, {"label": "I recognized my options but hesitated to use them", "target": "A1_D1", "signal": "axis1:internal"}, {"label": "I tried to change the situation and it still didn't work — but I tried", "target": "A1_D1", "signal": "axis1:internal"}, {"label": "I realized later I had more room to move than I thought at the time", "target": "A1_D1", "signal": "axis1:internal"}]

text: When you felt blocked today — what was the story you told yourself about why things weren't moving?
options: [{"label": "This system is set up in a way that makes progress hard", "target": "A1_D1", "signal": "axis1:external"}, {"label": "Other people's choices constrained what I could do", "target": "A1_D1", "signal": "axis1:external"}, {"label": "I didn't have the information or resources I needed", "target": "A1_D1", "signal": "axis1:external"}, {"label": "I wasn't sure if the block was real or if I was avoiding something harder", "target": "A1_D1", "signal": "axis1:internal"}]

text: [Route based on axis1 signal dominance]
Think about a moment today where something genuinely was outside your control. How did you carry that?
options: [{"label": "I accepted it and redirected my energy", "target": "A1_R2_INT", "signal": "axis1:internal"}, {"label": "I kept trying to solve what couldn't be solved — and it drained me", "target": "A1_R2_MID", "signal": "axis1:external"}, {"label": "I named it as a constraint and moved around it", "target": "A1_R2_INT", "signal": "axis1:internal"}, {"label": "I felt the frustration but didn't let it define the rest of the day", "target": "A1_R2_INT", "signal": "axis1:internal"}]

text: If you could rewind to one moment today where you felt stuck — and give yourself one honest piece of perspective — what would it be?
options: [{"label": "There was probably more I could have done — I just didn't see it then", "target": "A1_R2_MID", "signal": "axis1:internal"}, {"label": "I needed someone to help me and didn't ask", "target": "A1_R2_MID", "signal": "axis1:internal"}, {"label": "The situation really was constrained — but my reaction made it worse", "target": "A1_R2_MID", "signal": "axis1:external"}, {"label": "I don't think the outcome would have changed regardless of what I did", "target": "A1_R2_EXT", "signal": "axis1:external"}]

text: There's a specific kind of strength in letting go of what you can't control — not as resignation, but as a way of preserving energy for what you can. That's not passivity. That's discernment. You practiced it today, at least in part.

Seeing where your response added friction — even when the situation was genuinely hard — is one of the more uncomfortable forms of self-awareness. It doesn't mean you were wrong to feel what you felt. It means you're starting to separate the event from your interpretation of it.

Sometimes outcomes really don't change. What does change is what you learn about your own response patterns. Noticing that "I wouldn't have done anything differently" is itself data — it tells you something about where your ceiling of agency currently sits.
Axis 1 complete. You've looked at how you relate to your own agency today. Now we shift. The next axis isn't about what you could control — it's about what you chose to give.

Axis 2 of 3: Orientation. Every day at work, there's an invisible ledger. Some people are quietly tracking what they're owed. Others are thinking about what they can put in. Most of us do both — the question is which direction the weight falls.
When you think about the energy you spent today — what motivated most of it?
options: [{"label": "Getting something done that I cared about completing well", "target": "A2_R1_CONT", "signal": "axis2:contribution"}, {"label": "Making sure I was seen as contributing — recognition mattered to me today", "target": "A2_R1_ENT", "signal": "axis2:entitlement"}, {"label": "Helping someone else make progress, even when it slowed me down", "target": "A2_R1_CONT", "signal": "axis2:contribution"}, {"label": "Feeling like I was doing more than my share and not sure it was worth it", "target": "A2_R1_ENT", "signal": "axis2:entitlement"}, {"label": "A mix — some of each depending on the task", "target": "A2_R1_MID", "signal": "axis2:contribution"}]

text: Intrinsic motivation — caring about the work itself, or the person it helps — is the quiet engine of high-functioning teams. It doesn't announce itself. That's partly what makes it valuable: it doesn't depend on being seen.

The desire for recognition is human, and not inherently problematic. What's worth examining is whether the recognition became the goal — because when the ledger becomes "what I'm owed," effort gets contingent on return. That's a slow leak in both motivation and relationships.
Most people operate in this mixed space — some work feels meaningful, some feels transactional. The useful question isn't "which one am I?" but "which direction do I lean when things get hard?"
Was there a moment today where you did something that cost you — in time, energy, or credit — but you did it anyway?
options: [{"label": "Yes — and I don't regret it", "target": "A2_D1", "signal": "axis2:contribution"}, {"label": "Yes — and honestly, I'm a little resentful it went unnoticed", "target": "A2_D1", "signal": "axis2:entitlement"}, {"label": "No — I stayed within my lane today", "target": "A2_D1", "signal": "axis2:contribution"}, {"label": "I wanted to, but the cost felt too high given everything else", "target": "A2_D1", "signal": "axis2:contribution"}]

text: Think about a moment today when you felt the work wasn't being valued fairly. What did that feeling do to how you showed up afterward?
options: [{"label": "I pulled back — why give more if it won't be acknowledged?", "target": "A2_D1", "signal": "axis2:entitlement"}, {"label": "I kept going but it left a residue — harder to feel engaged", "target": "A2_D1", "signal": "axis2:entitlement"}, {"label": "I noticed the feeling and tried not to let it change my effort", "target": "A2_D1", "signal": "axis2:contribution"}, {"label": "I don't think I had that moment — or if I did, it didn't land hard", "target": "A2_D1", "signal": "axis2:contribution"}]

text: [Route based on axis2 signal dominance]
If the people you worked with today could describe your orientation — what's the most accurate thing they'd say?
options: [{"label": "She/he was trying to make things work for the team", "target": "A2_R2_CONT", "signal": "axis2:contribution"}, {"label": "She/he was focused and got their own work done", "target": "A2_R2_MID", "signal": "axis2:contribution"}, {"label": "Hard to tell — she/he was present but somewhat guarded", "target": "A2_R2_ENT", "signal": "axis2:entitlement"}, {"label": "She/he seemed frustrated about how things were going for them", "target": "A2_R2_ENT", "signal": "axis2:entitlement"}]

text: If you're honest — was there a moment today where you held something back that you could have offered? A piece of knowledge, a hand, an effort?
options: [{"label": "Yes — and looking back, I'm not sure that was the right call", "target": "A2_R2_MID", "signal": "axis2:contribution"}, {"label": "Yes — I was protecting my bandwidth and that felt necessary", "target": "A2_R2_ENT", "signal": "axis2:entitlement"}, {"label": "No — I gave what I had", "target": "A2_R2_CONT", "signal": "axis2:contribution"}, {"label": "I'm not sure — I didn't think about it in those terms at the time", "target": "A2_R2_MID", "signal": "axis2:contribution"}]

text: Contribution that isn't contingent on return is one of the rarer things in a workplace. It's not selfless in a naive way — it's a choice rooted in understanding that the environment you contribute to is also the environment you work inside.

There's a version of self-protection that's entirely rational — you can't give what you don't have. But there's another version that quietly calculates whether effort will be rewarded before extending it. It's worth knowing which one was operating today.

The gap between what we offered and what we could have offered is often invisible to us in the moment. Noticing it afterward — without judgment — is actually a form of growth. The question for tomorrow is: what would it have cost you to close that gap a little?

Axis 2 complete. You've examined the direction of your orientation today — inward or outward, receiving or giving. The final axis widens the frame further. Not just what you gave — but who you held in mind while you worked.

Axis 3 of 3: Radius. Maslow's final insight — the one most people skip — was that the highest form of human functioning isn't self-actualization. It's self-transcendence: the shift from "what do I need?" to "what does this moment, or this person, or this team need from me?"
When you think about the most significant moment of your day — who was in the picture?
options: [{"label": "Mainly me — it was about my work, my performance, my outcome", "target": "A3_R1_SELF", "signal": "axis3:self"}, {"label": "Me and a specific person — someone I was working with or for", "target": "A3_R1_MID", "signal": "axis3:others"}, {"label": "My team — what happened affected all of us", "target": "A3_R1_OTHERS", "signal": "axis3:others"}, {"label": "The people downstream — the customer, the user, whoever this work is ultimately for", "target": "A3_R1_OTHERS", "signal": "axis3:others"}, {"label": "I'm not sure — I didn't think about it in terms of who was affected", "target": "A3_R1_SELF", "signal": "axis3:self"}]

text: A self-referential frame isn't a flaw — it's often just the default when work is stressful. What's worth noticing is whether the frame stayed narrow all day, or whether there were moments when the picture naturally widened.

Holding another person in mind while you work — even one — is not a small thing. It changes the quality of the attention, and often the quality of the output. The question is whether that awareness extended further at any point today.

When the frame includes others — team, customers, the people downstream — the work takes on a different texture. The meaning isn't just in the task. It's in what the task is part of. That's not idealism; it's one of the most reliable sources of sustained engagement.
Was there a moment today where someone else's difficulty crossed your awareness — and you chose how to respond to that awareness?
options: [{"label": "I noticed it but stayed focused on my own work — I had too much going on", "target": "A3_D1", "signal": "axis3:self"}, {"label": "I did something about it, even briefly", "target": "A3_D1", "signal": "axis3:others"}, {"label": "I noticed it and felt guilty I didn't do more", "target": "A3_D1", "signal": "axis3:self"}, {"label": "I don't think I registered anyone else struggling today", "target": "A3_D1", "signal": "axis3:self"}]

text: Think about a moment today where you were aware of what someone else needed — not what they asked for, but what you sensed they needed. What did you do with that awareness?
options: [{"label": "I responded to it — I adjusted what I was doing to help", "target": "A3_D1", "signal": "axis3:others"}, {"label": "I noticed it and mentioned it, even if I couldn't fully address it", "target": "A3_D1", "signal": "axis3:others"}, {"label": "I filed it away — I wasn't in a position to act on it then", "target": "A3_D1", "signal": "axis3:self"}, {"label": "I didn't trust the read — I wasn't sure if I was projecting", "target": "A3_D1", "signal": "axis3:self"}]

text: [Route based on axis3 signal dominance]
If you zoom out to the purpose behind your work — what gave today's effort meaning, if anything did?
options: [{"label": "Completing things well — the quality mattered to me for its own sake", "target": "A3_R2_SELF", "signal": "axis3:self"}, {"label": "Moving something forward that other people are depending on", "target": "A3_R2_MID", "signal": "axis3:others"}, {"label": "Knowing the end user or customer benefits from this", "target": "A3_R2_OTHERS", "signal": "axis3:others"}, {"label": "I'm not sure today had a lot of meaning — it was more mechanical", "target": "A3_R2_SELF", "signal": "axis3:self"}]

text: When you were most effective today — what were you oriented toward?
options: [{"label": "Solving a problem that had been bothering me", "target": "A3_R2_SELF", "signal": "axis3:self"}, {"label": "Unblocking someone else so they could move forward", "target": "A3_R2_OTHERS", "signal": "axis3:others"}, {"label": "Producing something the team needed", "target": "A3_R2_OTHERS", "signal": "axis3:others"}, {"label": "Getting recognition for work I knew was solid", "target": "A3_R2_SELF", "signal": "axis3:self"}]

text: A self-contained frame of meaning is honest — not everyone connects their daily work to a larger purpose, and pretending to doesn't help. What's worth sitting with is whether there was a moment today where you glimpsed something beyond your own output — and if so, what it felt like.

The awareness of interdependence — that your work feeds into someone else's — is a quiet form of altruism. It doesn't require a grand sense of mission. Just the regular habit of asking: who needs this to land well, and what does that mean for how I approach it?

Orienting toward others — not as a performance, but as a genuine frame — is what Maslow identified as the least understood stage of development. It's not self-sacrifice. It's the discovery that the work becomes more meaningful when you're doing it for something larger than your own performance record.
One final question. Is there someone — a colleague, a direct report, a customer — whose experience of today you haven't fully considered yet?
options: [{"label": "Yes — and thinking about it now, I should probably follow up", "target": "A3_R3_OTHERS", "signal": "axis3:others"}, {"label": "Yes — I'm aware of it but I'm not sure what to do with it", "target": "A3_R3_MID", "signal": "axis3:others"}, {"label": "Not really — I think I was reasonably attentive to the people around me", "target": "A3_R3_OTHERS", "signal": "axis3:others"}, {"label": "I honestly haven't thought about anyone else's day today", "target": "A3_R3_SELF", "signal": "axis3:self"}]

text: That awareness — of someone whose experience you haven't fully considered — is the beginning of perspective-taking. Not the full act, just the opening move. It costs very little to carry that with you into tomorrow.

Not knowing what to do with an awareness doesn't make the awareness less valuable. Holding it without resolving it is sometimes the most honest response. The question for tomorrow is whether there's a small action — a message, a check-in — that would be better than continued uncertainty.

There's no accusation in noticing that today was self-contained. Sometimes that's what the day required. What's worth asking — just once, quickly — is whether there's anyone who might have needed something from you today that went unnoticed. Not as guilt. As data.

Today's reflection: You moved through three lenses. On agency — you leaned {axis1.dominant}, which meant you tended to {axis1.behavior}. On orientation — your energy ran more toward {axis2.dominant}, showing up as {axis2.behavior}. On radius — your awareness was centered on {axis3.dominant}, which shaped {axis3.behavior}. No single profile is the goal. The goal is to see clearly — so that tomorrow's choices are a little more intentional than today's. That's the whole practice.
Session complete. Same time tomorrow.
